topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the weight of a stone. My stomach immediately tightened, a cold knot forming just beneath my ribs. It was a notification from the publisher, confirming that my recent piece on urban gardening techniques had been published, but the byline listed was entirely wrong. It belonged to a complete stranger. I reread the text, my fingers trembling slightly over the screen. The words I had poured my late nights into, the unique insights I’d wrestled onto the page, were now attributed to someone else’s pen. A flush crept up my neck, warm and undeniable. I stared at the screen, unable to move, the silence of my small apartment amplifying the frantic drumming in my ears. How could this happen? It felt like a betrayal, a theft of my voice, and the sheer awkwardness of the situation made my throat feel impossibly dry.

[story 2]

I found the link buried deep in a promotional newsletter, and the sight of my article filled me with a sudden, nauseating lurch. My hands started to shake so badly I had to grip the edge of the desk. The name attached to the publication was unrecognizable—a name I’d never heard before. I clicked through, and there it was: my analysis of sustainable city planning, presented as if it were the brainchild of an established expert. A wave of heat washed over my face, making my skin prickle. I instinctively pulled my shoulders inward, hunching over, trying to make myself smaller, less visible. Every muscle in my body seemed to tense up, a defensive posture against the sudden, intense scrutiny of the situation. I wanted to disappear into the wallpaper.

[story 3]

When the notification popped up, I froze mid-sip of my morning coffee. The steam curled around my face, momentarily obscuring the dawning horror. My breath hitched, shallow and quick. I slowly lowered the mug, the ceramic cool against my palms. Reading the attribution was like watching someone else walk across my stage. A strange, hollow feeling settled in my chest, heavy and suffocating. I began pacing the length of my living room, my movements jerky and uncontrolled. Each step seemed loud in the quiet space, a physical manifestation of the unsettling feeling that everything I had created, everything that was uniquely mine, had been carelessly misplaced and claimed by another. I stopped abruptly by the window, staring out at the street, wishing the glass would just crack and let the whole embarrassing moment shatter.
